name: Frontend SPA Frameworks
description: Component-based single-page application development across the major reactive UI frameworks and their idiomatic patterns.
conventions: Keep components small, single-responsibility, and presentational where possible; lift shared state up or into a dedicated store rather than duplicating it across siblings.
Derive state instead of duplicating it; compute values from a single source of truth (memos/computed/derived) rather than syncing multiple state variables with effects.
Provide stable, unique keys for list rendering (never array indices for reorderable lists) so the framework can diff and preserve component identity correctly.
Treat effects as synchronization with external systems, not as a place for data derivation; always clean up subscriptions, timers, and listeners.
Avoid unnecessary re-renders by memoizing expensive computations and stable callbacks, and by keeping context/provider values narrow so consumers do not re-render on unrelated changes.
Prefer controlled, accessible form primitives and colocate validation with the form; debounce or batch expensive updates triggered by user input.
Type props, events, and store contracts explicitly and forbid implicit any at component boundaries.
Co-locate component, styles, tests, and stories, and split bundles with lazy/dynamic imports at route or heavy-component boundaries.
scaffolds: Scaffold a typed component with co-located unit test, story, and an exported public prop interface.
Generate a feature module (route + container + presentational components + store slice + tests) following the chosen framework's idioms.
Set up a global state store slice with typed actions, selectors, and devtools wiring.
Wire up a data-fetching hook with caching, loading/error states, and optimistic updates.

name: Full-Stack Meta-Frameworks & SSR
description: Server-rendered and hybrid meta-frameworks with routing, data loading, server components, and edge/serverless deployment.
conventions: Choose the rendering mode per route by data needs: static generation for stable content, server rendering for personalized/SEO-critical pages, client rendering only for interactive shells; use revalidation for content that changes periodically.
Fetch data on the server in loaders/server components and pass minimal serializable props to the client; never ship secrets or full database clients to the browser.
Keep server-only code clearly separated from client bundles using framework boundaries (server components, server files, route handlers).
Stream responses and use loading boundaries so above-the-fold content paints before slow data resolves.
Set explicit caching headers and revalidation strategies per data source; treat the cache as part of the contract and invalidate it on mutation.
Use the framework router's file-based conventions and nested layouts to avoid layout re-mounting and to share data fetching across nested routes.
Optimize images, fonts, and scripts through the framework's built-in primitives to protect Core Web Vitals.
Validate and sanitize all server-action/route-handler inputs with a schema, and return typed, structured errors.
scaffolds: Scaffold a new route with server-side data loading, a loading/error boundary, and SEO metadata.
Generate a server action / route handler with schema validation, auth check, and typed response.
Set up incremental static regeneration or on-demand revalidation for a content-driven route.
Bootstrap a deployment config for an edge/serverless target with environment variable wiring and caching headers.

name: Design Systems & Component Libraries
description: Reusable, accessible, themeable component libraries and design-token systems that enforce consistency across products.
conventions: Build on accessible headless primitives so keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA semantics are correct before styling is layered on.
Define a design-token layer (color, spacing, typography, radius, motion) as the single source of truth and reference tokens everywhere instead of hard-coded values.
Expose a small, composable API: prefer compound components and slots over large monolithic prop lists; allow consumers to override styles via class/style props or a theming API.
Document every component with stories for all states and an interaction/a11y addon, and treat the docs as the contract.
Version the library with semantic versioning, ship a changelog, and provide codemods or deprecation warnings for breaking API changes.
Support theming and dark mode through CSS variables or a theme provider rather than duplicating component variants.
Forward refs and spread remaining props to the underlying DOM element so components remain composable and accessible.
Test components for accessibility, visual regression, and behavior, and ship tree-shakeable, side-effect-free ES modules.
scaffolds: Scaffold a new accessible component built on a headless primitive with variants, tokens, story, and a11y test.
Generate a design-token pipeline producing CSS variables, a JS theme object, and platform outputs.
Set up a component-docs workspace with a11y, interactions, and visual-regression addons.
Create a theming provider with light/dark/system modes backed by CSS custom properties.

name: CSS & Styling Architecture
description: Modern CSS authoring with utility frameworks, CSS-in-JS, preprocessors, and scalable styling architecture.
conventions: Use modern responsive layout primitives (Flexbox, Grid, container queries, logical properties) and fluid sizing instead of fixed pixel breakpoints where possible.
Centralize design decisions in CSS custom properties or a token scale, and avoid magic numbers and one-off hard-coded colors.
Prefer zero-runtime or compiled styling for performance; reserve runtime CSS-in-JS for genuinely dynamic styles.
Scope styles to components to avoid global cascade leaks; use a naming methodology or scoping mechanism when writing plain CSS.
Respect user preferences with reduced-motion and color-scheme queries and accessible focus-visible styles; never remove focus outlines without a replacement.
Keep specificity low and flat; avoid important and deep descendant selectors that are hard to override.
Purge/tree-shake unused CSS in production and inline critical CSS for above-the-fold content to reduce render-blocking.
Ensure sufficient color contrast (WCAG AA) for text and interactive states directly in the token/theme definitions.
scaffolds: Scaffold a utility-CSS config with a custom design-token theme, dark mode, and content paths.
Set up a PostCSS pipeline (autoprefixer, nesting, custom-media, purge) for a build.
Generate a typed-token style sheet with responsive variants.
Create a responsive layout template using CSS Grid and container queries with fluid type.

name: Web Performance & Core Web Vitals
description: Measuring and optimizing load time, runtime performance, and Core Web Vitals across the rendering pipeline.
conventions: Optimize for Core Web Vitals: minimize Largest Contentful Paint with prioritized hero assets, keep Cumulative Layout Shift near zero with explicit dimensions, and reduce Interaction to Next Paint by breaking up long tasks.
Set width/height or aspect-ratio on media and reserve space for dynamically injected content to prevent layout shift.
Ship less JavaScript: code-split by route, lazy-load below-the-fold and heavy components, and tree-shake dependencies.
Serve modern, compressed assets: responsive images in AVIF/WebP with srcset, Brotli/gzip text compression, and subsetted, preloaded fonts with font-display swap.
Use resource hints deliberately (preload critical assets, preconnect to required origins, prefetch likely navigations) without over-hinting.
Cache aggressively with immutable hashed filenames, a Service Worker or CDN, and appropriate Cache-Control headers; defer or async non-critical third-party scripts.
Measure with field data (real-user monitoring) in addition to lab tools, and enforce performance budgets in CI to prevent regressions.
Avoid main-thread blocking by moving heavy work to Web Workers and virtualizing large lists.
scaffolds: Set up a performance-CI workflow with budgets that fail the build on regression.
Add real-user monitoring reporting LCP/CLS/INP to an analytics endpoint.
Generate a responsive-image component emitting AVIF/WebP srcset with correct sizes and dimensions.
Configure a bundle-analyzer plus code-splitting pass for a build to flag oversized chunks.

name: Web Accessibility (a11y / WCAG)
description: Building inclusive, WCAG-conformant interfaces with correct semantics, keyboard support, and assistive-tech compatibility.
conventions: Use native semantic HTML elements before reaching for ARIA; ARIA should supplement, not replace, correct semantics.
Ensure every interactive element is keyboard operable with a visible focus indicator and a logical tab order; manage focus on route changes, modals, and dynamically revealed content.
Associate form controls with labels, group related inputs, and surface validation errors programmatically with aria-describedby and aria-invalid.
Provide text alternatives: meaningful alt text for informative images, empty alt for decorative ones, captions/transcripts for media, and accessible names for icon-only controls.
Meet WCAG color-contrast minimums (AA: 4.5:1 text, 3:1 large text/UI) and never convey information by color alone.
Use ARIA live regions for asynchronous status updates and announce loading/error states to screen readers.
Respect reduced-motion, support 200% zoom and reflow without loss of content, and ensure touch targets are large enough.
Test with automated tooling plus manual keyboard and screen-reader passes, since automation catches only a subset of issues.
scaffolds: Add automated accessibility tests to the test suite and CI.
Scaffold an accessible modal/dialog with focus trap, escape handling, and ARIA roles.
Generate an accessible form with associated labels, error messaging, and live-region validation.
Create a skip-link plus landmark-region layout template with managed route focus.

name: Browser Extensions (WebExtensions / MV3)
description: Cross-browser extensions using the WebExtensions API and Manifest V3 with service workers, content scripts, and messaging.
conventions: Target Manifest V3 with a service-worker background that is event-driven and stateless; persist state to extension storage rather than relying on a long-lived background page.
Request the minimum permissions and host permissions needed, prefer activeTab and optional permissions, and justify each permission for store review.
Use the standardized cross-browser WebExtensions API via a polyfill instead of vendor-specific callback APIs.
Isolate content scripts from page scripts, inject styles in a way that avoids leaking into or breaking the host page, and clean up injected DOM and listeners.
Communicate between content scripts, background, and UI via typed message passing or a port; never trust messages without validating sender and shape.
Follow a strict Content Security Policy: no remote code execution, bundle all scripts locally, and avoid inline scripts and eval.
Store user data with the appropriate extension storage area, encrypt sensitive data, and document data usage for privacy and store policies.
Build with a framework or bundler that handles HMR, cross-browser manifests, and store packaging.
scaffolds: Scaffold an MV3 extension with background service worker, content script, and popup UI.
Set up typed message passing between content script, background, and popup with a shared schema.
Generate an options page persisting settings to extension storage with a cross-browser polyfill.
Configure cross-browser build and store-packaging with per-browser manifest variants.

name: Immersive Web (WebGL / WebGPU / WebXR / AR-VR)
description: 3D graphics, AR/VR, and GPU-accelerated experiences in the browser via Three.js, WebGL/WebGPU, and the WebXR API.
conventions: Reuse geometries, materials, and textures, and dispose of GPU resources explicitly on unmount to prevent memory leaks; instance repeated meshes rather than cloning.
Budget for the target device: cap draw calls and polygon counts, use compressed textures and geometry compression, and lazy-load assets progressively.
Drive animation from a single render/XR frame loop tied to a delta-time clock; pause rendering when the canvas is offscreen or the tab is hidden.
For WebXR, design for room-scale safety and comfort: stable framerate, avoid unintended camera acceleration, support both controllers and hand tracking, and gate entering VR/AR behind a user gesture.
Feature-detect WebGL2/WebGPU/WebXR support and provide graceful fallbacks when hardware or permissions are unavailable.
Manage color space, tone mapping, and physically based lighting consistently, and bake lighting where possible to reduce runtime cost.
Use a scene-graph abstraction instead of raw WebGL for maintainability, and keep CPU-side state separate from the render loop.
Provide accessible affordances for immersive content: alternative non-XR interactions, captions for audio, and motion-comfort options.
scaffolds: Scaffold a scene-graph render loop with orbit controls, lighting, and resource disposal.
Set up a WebXR session (VR/AR) with feature detection, enter-XR gate, and controller/hand input.
Generate a glTF model-viewer component with geometry/texture compression and progressive fallback.
Create a WebGPU compute/render pipeline scaffold with capability detection and WebGL fallback.

name: React Native Mobile Apps
description: Cross-platform iOS/Android apps built with React Native, including Expo-managed and bare workflows.
conventions: Prefer the managed workflow and cloud build/update tooling; only eject to a bare/prebuild workflow when a native module truly requires it, and keep config-as-code.
Adopt the New Architecture (Fabric renderer plus TurboModules/JSI) and a modern JS engine; gate legacy bridge-only native modules behind compatibility checks.
Run animations and gestures on the UI thread with worklet-based libraries instead of the JS-thread animation API to avoid frame drops.
Type the whole app, share a typed API client, and centralize navigation params in a typed param list.
Use windowed list components with stable key extractors and item layout hints rather than mapping over arrays in a scroll view.
Persist with a fast key-value store for small data and SQLite for relational data; never store secrets in plain async storage, use Keychain/Keystore wrappers.
Keep platform-specific code in platform-suffixed files or platform-select calls rather than scattering OS branches throughout components.
Wire crash/performance monitoring and end-to-end tests into CI before shipping to stores.
scaffolds: Scaffold a typed app with stack/tab navigators, theming, and cloud build/update profiles wired in.
Generate a typed API/data layer with a query cache, a store, and a fast persistence adapter.
Add an end-to-end test suite plus a CI matrix that runs lint, typecheck, unit tests, and preview builds.
Create a reusable native module template with iOS and Android bindings and a typed JS interface.

name: Flutter Apps
description: Cross-platform apps from a single Dart codebase targeting iOS, Android, web, and desktop with Flutter.
conventions: Structure the app in feature-first layers (presentation/application/domain/data) and keep widgets small, const, and free of business logic.
Pick one state-management approach and apply it consistently; expose immutable state and pure reducers/notifiers.
Use const constructors, keys, and builder-based lists/slivers for performance; avoid rebuilding large subtrees by scoping rebuilds narrowly.
Model immutable data with sealed/value classes and generate JSON serialization rather than hand-writing it.
Centralize theming via Material 3 color schemes and design tokens; support light/dark and dynamic color instead of hardcoding colors.
Handle async with builder widgets or async notifiers and always render loading/error/empty states explicitly.
Isolate platform code behind platform channels or federated plugins, and keep flavors/environments configured via compile-time defines and build flavors.
Enforce static analysis with a strict analysis config and cover logic with widget and integration tests in CI.
scaffolds: Scaffold a feature-first app with a state-management layer, declarative routing, Material 3 theming, and flavor/env configuration.
Generate a value-class data model plus repository and HTTP client with JSON serialization and typed error handling.
Set up golden, widget, and integration tests plus a CI workflow running analyze, test, and build for iOS/Android.
Create a federated plugin template exposing a platform channel with Android and iOS implementations.

name: Native iOS (Swift/SwiftUI)
description: First-class iOS/iPadOS apps built with Swift, SwiftUI, and modern Apple frameworks.
conventions: Build UI with SwiftUI first and drop to UIKit only where SwiftUI lacks coverage; bridge via representable wrappers rather than rewriting screens.
Adopt Swift Concurrency (async/await, actors, tasks) for asynchronous work and isolate mutable shared state in actors instead of callback pyramids.
Use the Observation framework for view models and keep views as pure functions of state.
Manage dependencies with Swift Package Manager, pin versions, and keep modules small with explicit public APIs for faster incremental builds.
Store secrets in the Keychain, never in user defaults; gate capabilities behind entitlements and request permissions just-in-time with clear usage strings.
Localize all user-facing strings via String Catalogs and support Dynamic Type, Dark Mode, and VoiceOver accessibility from the start.
Keep privacy usage descriptions accurate and declare required-reason APIs and a privacy manifest to satisfy App Store review.
Test with the platform test frameworks and UI tests, and automate builds/signing rather than manual archive steps.
scaffolds: Scaffold a SwiftUI app with a unidirectional architecture, dependency injection, and a typed navigation router.
Generate a networking layer using async/await with Codable models, retry/backoff, and a mockable protocol boundary.
Add a UI-test suite plus an automation lane for build, test, screenshots, and beta upload with managed signing.
Create a multi-module Swift Package workspace separating features, core, and UI components with explicit public interfaces.

name: Native Android (Kotlin/Compose)
description: Modern Android apps built with Kotlin, Jetpack Compose, and Android architecture components.
conventions: Build UI with Compose and unidirectional data flow: view models expose immutable UI state via observable streams and receive events, composables stay stateless and hoist state.
Follow a layered architecture (UI / domain / data) with repositories abstracting data sources behind interfaces.
Use coroutines and flows for async work, scoping collection with lifecycle-aware collectors to avoid leaks.
Inject dependencies with a DI framework instead of manual singletons, and keep modules build-isolated for speed.
Persist relational data with a typed database layer and key-value/settings with a modern datastore, not legacy shared preferences for new code.
Centralize theming with Material 3 and dynamic color, support dark theme, and externalize all strings/dimens into resources for localization.
Target a recent SDK level, request runtime permissions just-in-time, and respect scoped storage and background-execution limits.
Enforce linting/static analysis and run unit, Compose UI, and instrumented tests in CI; manage versions via a version catalog.
scaffolds: Scaffold a multi-module Compose app with DI, a view model plus state-stream pattern, declarative navigation, and Material 3 theming.
Generate a data layer with an HTTP client plus serialization, a local database, and a repository exposing flows.
Add Compose UI tests, unit tests, and a CI workflow running static analysis, assemble, and instrumented tests on an emulator.
Create a build-convention-plugin and version-catalog setup to standardize build logic across feature modules.

name: Hybrid / WebView Mobile (Ionic & Capacitor)
description: Web-tech mobile apps that wrap a web UI in native shells using Capacitor/Cordova with Ionic or any web framework.
conventions: Prefer a modern native-bridge runtime over legacy hybrid frameworks for new projects; access native capabilities through official/community plugins with a consistent JS bridge.
Keep the web bundle small and lazy-loaded; hybrid startup is dominated by WebView load time, so code-split routes and defer non-critical work.
Use platform-adaptive components and CSS variables for theming so the same UI feels native on both iOS and Android.
Commit the web layer to source control but treat native projects as managed; sync via the tooling rather than hand-editing generated files.
Guard native plugin calls behind a platform check so the same codebase degrades gracefully when run as a PWA in the browser.
Handle safe-area insets, hardware back button, and keyboard resize behavior explicitly since WebViews do not get these for free.
Store sensitive data with secure-storage/Keychain plugins, not local storage, and configure a strict Content Security Policy for the WebView.
scaffolds: Scaffold a hybrid app with platform-adaptive routing, CSS-variable theming, and iOS/Android platforms added.
Generate a native-plugin wrapper that exposes a typed API and provides a browser fallback for PWA builds.
Set up a CI pipeline that builds the web bundle, syncs native projects, and produces signed iOS/Android artifacts.

name: Electron Desktop Apps
description: Cross-platform desktop apps built on Chromium plus Node.js with Electron and a web-based UI.
conventions: Enable context isolation and sandboxing, disable node integration in renderers, and expose only a minimal, validated API surface via the preload bridge.
Treat the main process as a privileged backend: keep file/system/network access there and communicate over typed, validated IPC channels rather than exposing Node to the UI.
Validate and allow-list every IPC message and never pass raw user input into shell/exec; set a strict Content Security Policy and disable the remote module.
Ship auto-updates with code-signed and notarized builds for each platform.
Bundle and tree-shake renderer code, and lazy-load heavy features to keep startup memory and time reasonable.
Externalize native Node modules correctly and rebuild them against the bundled runtime ABI instead of the system Node.
Persist app data in the per-user data directory with versioned migrations, and instrument crash reporting per process.
Test main and renderer separately and add end-to-end coverage with a driver that supports Electron.
scaffolds: Scaffold an Electron app with a bundler, a secure preload using the context bridge, typed IPC, and packaging for win/mac/linux.
Generate an auto-update pipeline with code signing, notarization, and a releases publish step.
Add an end-to-end test harness plus crash reporting wired into main and renderer processes.
Create a typed IPC layer with request/response validation and a shared schema between main and renderer.

name: Lightweight Native Desktop (Tauri / Wails / Slint)
description: Small, fast desktop apps using OS-native webviews or native toolkits with Rust/Go backends.
conventions: Keep privileged operations (filesystem, network, shell) in the native core and expose them to the webview only through explicit, narrowly-scoped commands.
Lock down the capability allowlist and CSP so the frontend can call only the commands it actually needs, following least privilege.
Validate and type all command inputs crossing the JS-to-native boundary; treat the webview as untrusted and never interpolate input into shell calls.
Leverage the OS-provided webview to keep binaries small, and account for per-platform webview behavioral differences.
Use the framework's updater with signed update artifacts and public-key verification rather than rolling a custom update mechanism.
Code-sign and notarize per platform and produce platform-native installers.
Keep the native core testable with unit tests and run the full bundle build in CI across macOS, Windows, and Linux runners.
scaffolds: Scaffold an app with a typed command bridge, scoped capabilities/CSP, a web frontend, and cross-platform bundle configuration.
Generate a signed auto-updater setup with key generation, update manifest, and CI publishing.
Add a cross-platform CI matrix that builds, signs, and notarizes installers for macOS, Windows, and Linux.
Create a native command module with input validation, error mapping to the frontend, and unit tests.

name: Qt / C++ Cross-Platform Desktop
description: High-performance native desktop and embedded apps using the Qt framework with C++ or QML.
conventions: Build UI with Qt Quick/QML for modern fluid interfaces or Qt Widgets for classic desktop apps; keep business logic in C++ and expose it to QML via context properties or registered types.
Use the signal/slot mechanism and parent-based ownership for memory management instead of manual new/delete, and prefer smart pointers at API boundaries.
Keep the GUI thread responsive by moving long-running work to worker threads, communicating results back via queued signals.
Target a current Qt version with CMake as the build system, and structure platform-specific code behind Qt's abstractions rather than ifdef sprawl.
Mark user-facing strings for translation and ship translation files; respect system theme, high-DPI scaling, and accessibility.
Be deliberate about licensing: ship under LGPL with dynamic linking (or hold a commercial license) and document third-party attributions.
Package per platform with the deployment tooling and test on each target OS in CI.
scaffolds: Scaffold a Qt plus CMake project with a QML frontend, a C++ backend registered to QML, and a model exposed to the view.
Generate a worker-thread pattern with signal/slot result delivery for background tasks.
Set up translation extraction and a cross-platform deployment/packaging step in CI.

name: Progressive Web Apps & Offline-First
description: Installable, offline-capable web apps using service workers, web app manifests, and modern web platform APIs.
conventions: Ship a complete web app manifest (name, icons in multiple sizes, theme color, display, start URL) and serve over HTTPS so the app is installable.
Generate and version service workers with a build tool rather than hand-writing caching logic, and adopt explicit precache plus runtime-cache strategies.
Choose caching strategy per request type (cache-first for static assets, network-first or stale-while-revalidate for API data) and never cache opaque/auth responses blindly.
Handle service-worker lifecycle deliberately: prompt the user to refresh on a new version instead of silently breaking an open session, and clean up stale caches on activate.
Design offline-first with an IndexedDB wrapper for structured data and a background-sync queue for deferred writes.
Treat advanced capabilities (push, share, file access) as progressive enhancements behind feature detection, with graceful fallbacks where unsupported.
Budget and measure Core Web Vitals, and serve responsive images and code-split bundles.
Request notification/persistent-storage permissions just-in-time with clear value, never on first load.
scaffolds: Scaffold a PWA with a build-tool service worker, a complete manifest with generated icons, and an install-prompt plus update-available flow.
Generate an offline-first data layer using IndexedDB plus a background-sync write queue.
Add a performance-budget check to the build pipeline.
Create per-route runtime caching strategies with cache versioning and cleanup.

name: Kotlin Multiplatform & Compose Multiplatform
description: Shared business logic (and optionally UI) across Android, iOS, desktop, and web with Kotlin Multiplatform.
conventions: Maximize shared common code (models, networking, repositories, use cases) and keep platform-specific implementations behind expect/actual declarations.
Use multiplatform-ready libraries for networking, serialization, persistence, and concurrency instead of JVM-only dependencies in shared code.
Expose a clean, suspend/flow-based API to consumers; on iOS wrap suspend functions for Swift-friendly async rather than leaking coroutine internals.
Decide UI strategy explicitly: share UI with Compose Multiplatform, or keep native UI per platform and share only logic; do not half-do both.
Inject dependencies with a multiplatform DI framework and keep the shared module free of platform framework imports.
Structure as a multi-module build with a version catalog, and run shared tests in common test plus platform-specific instrumentation.
Package the shared module as a framework for iOS consumers and a Gradle dependency for Android.
scaffolds: Scaffold a project with shared common code (networking, serialization, persistence), Android and iOS targets, and DI.
Generate expect/actual platform abstractions for storage, networking, and platform info with implementations per target.
Set up a shared-UI module or native consumers plus a framework export for iOS.
Add common-module unit tests and a CI matrix building Android, iOS, and desktop targets.

name: Backend APIs (REST / GraphQL / gRPC)
description: Steers toward well-versioned, contract-first HTTP/RPC service APIs with consistent error handling, pagination, and auth.
conventions: Design contract-first: define the OpenAPI/Protobuf/GraphQL schema before writing handlers, and generate types/clients from it so the contract is the single source of truth.
Version the public API explicitly and never make breaking changes within a published version; deprecate with a sunset window instead.
Return structured, machine-readable errors with a stable code, human message, and correlation id; map domain errors to correct status codes rather than a generic 500.
Use cursor-based pagination for large collections instead of offset/limit to keep results stable under concurrent writes, and cap page size with a documented maximum.
Validate and sanitize all inbound payloads at the boundary against the schema, rejecting unknown fields where appropriate, and never trust client-supplied ids for authorization.
Make mutating endpoints idempotent where possible (idempotency keys for create, natural replace semantics) so retries from clients and gateways are safe.
Enforce authentication and authorization as middleware/interceptors, prefer short-lived bearer tokens with scope or role checks, and apply rate limiting per principal.
Keep handlers thin: push business logic into a service layer and keep transport adapters separate so the same domain logic is reusable across protocols.
scaffolds: Scaffold an OpenAPI-first REST service: spec file, generated server stubs and typed client, request validation, and a standardized error envelope.
Scaffold a gRPC service from a proto: code-gen wiring, interceptors for auth/logging/metrics, health and reflection services, and a REST gateway.
Scaffold a GraphQL API: schema, resolver skeletons with batching to avoid N+1, query depth/complexity limits, and persisted queries.
Generate a versioning plus deprecation checklist task that diffs a new schema against the published one and flags breaking changes.

name: Databases & Data Modeling
description: Steers toward sound relational/NoSQL schema design, safe migrations, query performance, and correct transaction/consistency handling.
conventions: Treat schema changes as versioned, ordered, forward-only migrations checked into source control; pair each with a tested rollback or an expand/contract plan, never edit applied migrations.
Do schema evolution in expand-then-contract phases (add nullable column/backfill/dual-write, switch reads, then drop) so deploys stay backward-compatible and zero-downtime.
Index to match real query predicates and sort orders; add composite and covering indexes deliberately, and verify with query plans rather than guessing, since unused indexes only slow writes.
Choose the narrowest correct column types and add constraints at the database layer so the data model enforces invariants, not just application code.

Pick the right transaction isolation level for the workload and keep transactions short; understand the anomalies your level allows and guard against lost updates with locks or optimistic version columns.
Always parameterize queries to prevent injection and improve plan reuse; never build SQL by string concatenation of user input.
Use connection pooling sized to the database's limits and avoid per-request connection churn; set sane statement timeouts so a slow query cannot exhaust the pool.
Avoid N+1 access patterns by batching/joining or using a loader; for high-read paths consider read replicas and accept the replication lag explicitly in the design.
scaffolds: Scaffold a migration framework setup: ordered up/down migration files, a CI check that runs migrate-up on a throwaway DB, and a seed-data script.
Generate an expand/contract migration plan for a column rename or type change, including backfill batching and dual-write/read-switch steps.
Scaffold a typed data-access layer from SQL query files with parameterized queries.
Create an index-and-query audit task that runs query plans on the slowest queries and proposes indexes.

name: DevOps & Infrastructure as Code
description: Steers toward declarative, reviewable, idempotent infrastructure with state safety, modularity, and least-privilege provisioning.
conventions: Declare infrastructure declaratively and keep it in version control; the repo is the source of truth and changes flow through pull requests with a plan/diff reviewed before apply.
Always review a dry-run plan before applying, and apply through automation rather than from individual laptops.
Store remote state with locking and encryption so concurrent applies cannot corrupt it; never commit state files or secrets, and treat state as sensitive.
Make operations idempotent and convergent so re-running produces no spurious changes; avoid imperative one-off scripts that drift from declared state.
Compose infrastructure from small, versioned, reusable modules with clear inputs/outputs; pin module and provider versions for reproducible builds.
Parameterize per-environment via variable files or workspaces rather than copy-pasting, keeping environments structurally identical to catch drift.
Provision with least privilege: scope service accounts/roles to exactly what a component needs, and inject secrets from a vault/secret manager at apply time, never hardcoded.
Detect and remediate drift continuously and tag all resources with owner, environment, and cost-center for governance.
scaffolds: Scaffold an IaC project with a remote backend plus state locking, per-environment workspaces, and a reusable module layout.
Generate a CI pipeline that runs format/validate/lint and posts a plan diff on PRs, gating apply behind approval on the main branch.
Scaffold a configuration-management playbook plus inventory with check-mode support, roles, and idempotent tasks.
Create a drift-detection task that runs a scheduled plan and reports unmanaged or changed resources.

name: Cloud-Native & Container Orchestration
description: Steers toward containerized, Kubernetes-native, twelve-factor workloads with healthchecks, resource limits, autoscaling, and GitOps delivery.
conventions: Build stateless, twelve-factor workloads: configuration via environment/secrets, no local disk for durable state, and graceful shutdown on termination signals so pods can be rescheduled freely.
Define liveness, readiness, and startup probes distinctly so the orchestrator restarts hung pods, withholds traffic until ready, and tolerates slow boots.
Set CPU/memory requests and limits on every container so the scheduler can bin-pack and so a runaway pod cannot starve neighbors; right-size from observed usage.
Use small, pinned, multi-stage container images running as a non-root user with a read-only root filesystem and dropped capabilities to shrink the attack surface.
Deliver via GitOps: a git repo is the desired state and a controller reconciles the cluster to it, making deploys auditable and rollbacks a git revert.
Externalize config and secrets into config/secret objects (or a secrets operator) and inject at runtime; never bake credentials into images.
Roll out progressively (rolling/blue-green/canary) with health-gated promotion and automatic rollback, and use disruption budgets to keep capacity during node drains.
Apply network policies, namespaces, and role-based access control for least-privilege isolation between workloads and the control plane.
scaffolds: Scaffold a multi-stage container image (non-root, minimal base, pinned digests) plus an ignore file for a given language.
Generate a Kubernetes deployment set: workload with probes/limits, service, autoscaler, and a chart or overlay per environment.
Scaffold a GitOps repo layout wired to a reconciling controller.
Create a progressive-delivery manifest with health-gated promotion and rollback.

name: CI/CD Pipelines & Release Automation
description: Steers toward fast, reproducible, secure pipelines: cached builds, gated tests, artifact provenance, and automated, reversible deploys.
conventions: Make builds reproducible and hermetic: pin tool and dependency versions via lockfiles, and avoid pipeline steps that depend on hidden machine state so the same commit always yields the same artifact.
Fail fast and run independent stages in parallel; order cheap checks before expensive ones so feedback is quick.
Cache dependencies and build layers keyed by lockfile hash to cut pipeline time, but key caches correctly so stale caches never mask broken builds.
Build an artifact once and promote that same immutable artifact through environments by digest, rather than rebuilding per stage, to guarantee what was tested is what ships.
Gate merges on required status checks and keep the main branch always releasable; block on test, lint, and security-scan results.
Never hardcode secrets in pipeline files; inject from the platform's secret store with least-privilege, short-lived credentials and prefer federated identity over long-lived keys.
Scan in the pipeline (dependency, static analysis, container image, IaC) and generate provenance/SBOM so releases are auditable and supply-chain-verifiable.
Automate deploys with a clear rollback path (versioned releases, health checks, automatic revert on failure) so any deploy can be undone quickly.
scaffolds: Scaffold a CI workflow with cached deps, parallel lint/test jobs, matrix builds, and required-check gating.
Generate a build-once/promote pipeline that pushes an immutable image and deploys the same digest across dev/staging/prod.
Scaffold federated-identity cloud auth in CI to replace long-lived access keys.
Create a release-automation task: semantic version bump, changelog generation, SBOM/provenance, and a tagged release.

name: Observability & Monitoring
description: Steers toward instrumented services with metrics, structured logs, and distributed traces tied to SLOs and actionable alerts.
conventions: Instrument the three signals together: metrics for aggregate health, structured logs for events, and distributed traces for request flow, and correlate them via a shared trace/request id.
Use vendor-neutral instrumentation and semantic conventions so telemetry is portable and exporters/backends can be swapped without recoding.
Emit structured logs, not free-text, with consistent fields (timestamp, level, service, trace id) so they are queryable and machine-parseable.
Propagate trace context across service boundaries and message queues so a single request can be followed end-to-end through the whole system.
Define service-level indicators and objectives and alert on symptoms that affect users (latency, error rate, saturation) rather than on every raw metric, to keep alerts actionable.
Prefer low-cardinality metric labels; never put unbounded values into metric labels, as cardinality explosion can overwhelm the metrics backend.
Make telemetry safe and affordable: redact secrets and personal data from logs and span attributes, and sample traces on high-volume paths.
Build dashboards and alerts as version-controlled code and include runbook links in alerts so on-call responders have immediate context.
scaffolds: Scaffold instrumentation for a service: SDK setup, auto-instrumentation, an exporter, and context propagation across HTTP/RPC.
Generate a structured-logging setup with correlation/trace-id injection and sensitive-data redaction.
Scaffold a metrics plus alerting bundle: instrumented counters/histograms, scrape config, and objective-based alert rules with runbook links.
Create dashboards-as-code for rate/errors/duration and utilization/saturation/errors methods.

name: Message Queues & Event Streaming
description: Steers toward reliable async messaging: durable delivery, idempotent consumers, ordering/partitioning, and dead-letter handling.
conventions: Make consumers idempotent: assume at-least-once delivery and deduplicate by message id or use idempotency keys, because exactly-once is hard and most brokers redeliver on failure.
Acknowledge messages only after successful processing (and commit offsets after, not before) so a crash mid-processing causes redelivery rather than silent loss.
Route messages that repeatedly fail to a dead-letter queue with a retry/backoff policy, so a single poison message cannot block the partition or stall the consumer group.
Preserve ordering only where required by using a partition/routing key; accept that strict global ordering limits parallelism and design for per-key ordering instead.
Version event schemas and evolve them compatibly; add fields rather than repurposing them so old consumers keep working during rollout.
Decouple producers and consumers via the broker and keep messages self-contained or use the claim-check pattern for large payloads.
Use the transactional-outbox or change-data-capture pattern to publish events atomically with the database write, avoiding dual-write inconsistency.
Monitor consumer lag, queue depth, and redelivery counts, and alert when consumers fall behind so backpressure is visible before it becomes an outage.
scaffolds: Scaffold a producer/consumer pair with idempotent processing, manual ack/offset commit, and retry-with-backoff plus dead-letter routing.
Generate a transactional-outbox plus change-data-capture setup that publishes domain events atomically with DB writes.
Scaffold a schema-registry-backed event contract with compatible-evolution checks in CI.
Create a consumer-lag monitoring plus alerting task for a topic/queue.

name: Caching & In-Memory Stores
description: Steers toward correct, safe caching: explicit invalidation, TTLs, stampede protection, and the right cache topology for the workload.
conventions: Set an explicit TTL on every cache entry so stale data self-heals; never cache indefinitely without an invalidation or expiry strategy, as unbounded staleness is a correctness bug.
Pick an invalidation strategy deliberately (TTL expiry, write-through, or explicit delete-on-write) and design for it up front, since cache invalidation is genuinely hard.
Protect against cache stampede on hot keys: use request coalescing/single-flight, jittered TTLs, or early/background refresh so a key expiry does not flood the origin with identical recomputations.
Choose the topology to match the access pattern: cache-aside for read-heavy with tolerable misses, write-through/write-behind when reads must be fresh, and size the cache so the working set fits.
Treat the cache as a performance optimization, not a source of truth: the system must remain correct (just slower) if the cache is empty or unavailable, and must tolerate cache nodes failing.
Namespace and version cache keys so a deploy that changes the cached shape can invalidate cleanly without serving incompatible data.
Bound memory with an eviction policy appropriate to the workload, and monitor hit rate, evictions, and memory pressure to know whether the cache is actually helping.
Guard against thundering-herd misses and penetration of nonexistent keys by caching negative results briefly or using a bloom filter to shield the backing store.
scaffolds: Scaffold a cache-aside helper with TTL plus jitter, single-flight stampede protection, and hit/miss/eviction metrics.
Generate a write-through/write-behind caching layer wired to a data-access repository.
Scaffold versioned, namespaced cache-key helpers tied to the deployed schema version.
Create a cache-health monitoring task reporting hit rate, eviction rate, and memory usage with alert thresholds.

name: Classical ML & Data Science
description: Tabular data analysis, feature engineering, and classical (non-deep) machine learning with the data-science stack.
conventions: Split data before any fitting and keep a held-out test set untouched; use cross-validation only on the train split to avoid leakage.
Wrap preprocessing and the estimator in a single pipeline so transforms are fit only on training folds and applied identically at inference.
Fit scalers, encoders, and imputers on training data only; never fit on the full dataset or on the test set.
Select metrics that match the problem and class balance rather than relying on accuracy alone.
Set and record seeds for reproducible splits, models, and resampling.
Prefer vectorized array operations over row loops; use categorical dtypes and chunking for large frames.
Tune hyperparameters with nested or grouped cross-validation when appropriate, and report variance across folds, not a single point estimate.
Check for and document data leakage, target leakage, and train/serve skew before trusting results.
scaffolds: End-to-end tabular pipeline scaffold: load, split, transform, model, cross-validate, and persist an artifact.
Exploratory-analysis notebook template with profiling, missingness/outlier checks, and correlation/leakage diagnostics.
Hyperparameter search task with logged trials and a reproducibility seed block.
Model card plus evaluation report generator summarizing metrics, CV variance, and feature importances.

name: Deep Learning Frameworks & Training
description: Building, training, and scaling neural networks with the major frameworks and high-level training loops.
conventions: Seed all RNGs and set deterministic flags when reproducibility is required, accepting the performance trade-off.
Keep data loading off the critical path with prefetching, pinned memory, and enough workers; profile before assuming the accelerator is the bottleneck.
Use mixed precision and gradient accumulation to fit larger effective batch sizes, and scale learning rate with batch size.
Separate model definition, training loop, data module, and config so experiments are composable and resumable from checkpoints.
Always evaluate on a validation set each epoch, checkpoint the best model by the validation metric, and use early stopping to avoid overfitting.
Move tensors and models to the correct device explicitly and avoid host-device syncs inside hot loops.
Clip gradients and monitor for NaN/Inf losses; log learning rate, gradient norm, and throughput, not just loss.
Prefer established distributed strategies over hand-rolled multi-accelerator code, and verify single-device correctness first.
scaffolds: Training scaffold with config, data module, checkpointing, and resume support.
Mixed-precision plus gradient-accumulation training loop template with metric logging hooks.
Distributed launch script with per-rank logging.
Experiment config plus sweep template wiring seeds, callbacks, and artifact paths.

name: NLP & LLM Applications
description: RAG, agents, prompt orchestration, and LLM-powered application development with retrieval and tool use.
conventions: Treat prompts as versioned, reviewable artifacts; separate system/instruction/context/user roles and keep templates out of inline string concatenation.
In retrieval-augmented generation, chunk with semantic boundaries and overlap, store rich metadata for filtering, and evaluate retrieval separately from generation quality.
Ground answers in retrieved context and require citations; instruct the model to abstain when context is insufficient rather than hallucinate.
Constrain and validate model output with structured schemas (JSON/function calling) and parse defensively with retries on malformed output.
Set token budgets, timeouts, and cost/rate-limit handling; stream responses and cache deterministic calls where possible.
Never inject untrusted content directly as instructions; mitigate prompt injection by isolating retrieved/user data from system directives.
Build an evaluation harness with a fixed test set and automatic graders before tuning prompts.
Keep API keys out of code, abstract providers behind a single client, and log requests/responses for traceability without leaking sensitive data.
scaffolds: Retrieval pipeline scaffold: ingest, chunk, embed, store, retrieve, and produce a grounded answer with citations.
Evaluation harness template with a golden dataset, judge prompts, and regression scoring.
Tool-calling agent skeleton with typed tool schemas, retry/guardrail wrapping, and tracing.
Structured-extraction task: schema definition plus validation and repair loop over model output.

name: Computer Vision
description: Image/video classification, detection, segmentation, and vision pipelines with deep and classical methods.
conventions: Apply data augmentation on the training set only and keep validation/test transforms deterministic.
Match normalization and input resolution to the pretrained backbone's expectations when fine-tuning.
Choose task-appropriate metrics (mAP/IoU for detection and segmentation, top-k accuracy for classification) and report per-class results.
Verify annotation correctness and coordinate conventions before training to avoid silent label errors.
Use transfer learning from pretrained weights for small datasets, and freeze/unfreeze layers progressively.
Handle class imbalance and small objects explicitly with appropriate losses, tiling, or weighted sampling.
Decouple decoding/post-processing from the model and tune confidence/overlap thresholds on validation data.
Profile the input pipeline since decode/resize/augment is often the throughput bottleneck, not the accelerator.
scaffolds: Detection/segmentation training scaffold with dataset adapter, augmentation pipeline, and mAP/IoU evaluation.
Transfer-learning image-classification template with pretrained backbone and progressive unfreezing.
Inference plus visualization task that overlays boxes/masks and exports to an optimized runtime format.
Dataset conversion utility between common annotation formats with label-sanity checks.

name: Scientific Computing & Numerical Methods
description: Numerical computing, simulation, optimization, and symbolic/statistical modeling for research and engineering.
conventions: Vectorize with array operations and broadcasting; reserve explicit loops for cases that cannot be expressed in array form, and consider JIT compilation for those.
Be explicit about dtypes and precision and understand floating-point error accumulation and conditioning in numerical algorithms.
Prefer well-tested library solvers over hand-rolled numerics, and check convergence/residuals rather than assuming success.
Use stable formulations and check matrix conditioning before inverting.
Validate numerical code against analytical solutions, conservation laws, or convergence-order studies, not just that it runs.
Track and propagate units and physical constants explicitly; keep dimensional consistency in formulas.
Set tolerances, max iterations, and seeds explicitly for iterative and stochastic methods so results are reproducible.
Profile and report complexity/memory; exploit sparsity and structure instead of dense general routines.
scaffolds: Numerical experiment scaffold with a solver, convergence/order-of-accuracy study, and plots.
Vectorized-vs-loop benchmark template comparing array and JIT implementations.
Simulation skeleton with a configurable solver, boundary conditions, and validation against a known solution.
Optimization task template with gradient checks and convergence diagnostics.

name: Bioinformatics & Genomics
description: Sequence analysis, genomics/omics pipelines, and reproducible biological data workflows.
conventions: Use standard file formats and their indexed forms and validate them rather than parsing ad hoc.
Pin tool versions and reference genome/annotation builds; record them so analyses are reproducible and comparable across runs.
Build pipelines with workflow managers and containers for portability and provenance.
Apply appropriate QC and filtering at each stage before downstream analysis.
Correct for multiple testing in differential/association analyses and report adjusted p-values and effect sizes.
Be explicit about coordinate systems (0-based vs 1-based, half-open intervals) and strand conventions to avoid off-by-one errors.
Stream and chunk large genomic files instead of loading them fully into memory, and use the indexed access APIs.
Document the reference, parameters, and software environment for every step to support reproducible science.
scaffolds: Workflow-manager pipeline scaffold with containerized steps, config, and per-stage QC reports.
Sequence-analysis task with format validation and indexed access.
Single-cell analysis template covering QC, normalization, clustering, and marker genes.
Variant-calling/annotation workflow skeleton with reference pinning and filtering thresholds.

name: Data Engineering & Pipelines
description: Batch/stream ETL/ELT, orchestration, and large-scale data processing with reliable, tested pipelines.
conventions: Make pipeline steps idempotent and safe to retry; design for at-least-once delivery and deduplicate downstream.
Prefer declarative, observable orchestration with explicit dependencies, retries, backfills, and SLAs over cron-driven scripts.
Validate data quality at boundaries with schema/contract and expectation checks, and fail fast on contract violations.
Use columnar formats and partitioning/predicate pushdown for analytical workloads; avoid row-by-row processing at scale.
Separate compute from storage and parameterize environments so the same pipeline runs across dev/staging/prod.
Capture lineage and metadata, and make jobs deterministic given the same inputs and parameters for reproducible reruns.
Handle late, missing, and malformed data explicitly with dead-letter paths and watermarks for streaming.
Test transformations with unit tests on logic and integration tests on representative samples, not only in production.
scaffolds: Orchestrated ETL/ELT scaffold with idempotent tasks, retries, and backfill support.
Transformation project template with staging/mart models, tests, and documentation.
Batch processing job reading and writing partitioned columnar data with schema checks.
Data-quality gate task wiring expectation/schema contracts into the pipeline.

name: MLOps, Experiment Tracking & Serving
description: Experiment tracking, model registry, deployment/serving, and monitoring for production ML systems.
conventions: Track every experiment's params, metrics, code version, data version, and environment so any run is reproducible and comparable.
Version data and artifacts (not just code) and register models with stage/lifecycle metadata in a model registry.
Package models with their preprocessing and dependencies behind a stable inference contract to prevent train/serve skew.
Automate the path from training to deployment with CI/CD, and gate promotion on evaluation metrics and tests rather than manual copying.
Monitor production models for data drift, prediction drift, latency, and quality, with alerting and a rollback/canary strategy.
Keep feature computation consistent between training and serving, ideally via a shared feature pipeline or feature store.
Make deployments reproducible and rollback-able with pinned images, infrastructure-as-code, and immutable artifacts.
Define latency/throughput/availability objectives and load-test serving before launch; scale with batching/autoscaling as needed.
scaffolds: Experiment-tracking plus model-registry scaffold logging params, metrics, and signed artifacts.
Model serving template with input schema validation and a health/metrics endpoint.
CI/CD pipeline for ML: train, evaluate, gate, register, deploy with versioned artifacts.
Drift/quality monitoring task producing reports and alert thresholds for a deployed model.

name: Game Development
description: Building games and interactive experiences with engines, ECS architectures, and tight game loops.
conventions: Keep the game loop deterministic by decoupling the fixed-timestep simulation/physics update from the variable-rate render loop, and interpolate render state between simulation ticks.
Multiply per-frame movement, timers, and physics by delta time so behavior is frame-rate independent across machines.
Prefer data-oriented design and Entity-Component-System layouts over deep inheritance hierarchies to keep hot data cache-friendly and systems composable.
Pool and reuse frequently spawned objects instead of allocating per frame to avoid GC pauses and allocator churn in the hot path.
Separate game logic from rendering and input so the simulation can run headless for tests, deterministic replays, and dedicated servers.
Drive content (levels, tuning values, spawn tables) from data/assets rather than hardcoding, so designers iterate without recompiling.
For networked games use server-authoritative simulation with client-side prediction and reconciliation; never trust client-reported state.
Budget per-frame work to the target frame time and profile with a frame profiler rather than guessing at bottlenecks.
scaffolds: Scaffold an ECS-based game skeleton with fixed-timestep update loop, render interpolation, and an input abstraction layer.
Generate a 2D platformer starter: tilemap loader, collision/resolution, sprite animation state machine, and camera follow.
Set up a client/server netcode template with snapshot interpolation, client prediction, and server reconciliation.
Create an asset/content pipeline task: import textures and audio, generate atlases, and emit a runtime manifest.

name: 3D Graphics & Modeling
description: Mesh processing, geometry, scene/asset pipelines, content-creation tooling, and 3D content formats.
conventions: Adopt a single, explicit coordinate-system and handedness convention (axis up, winding order, units) at import time and convert all assets into it.
Prefer indexed triangle meshes with shared vertex buffers over duplicated vertices to cut memory and improve vertex-cache reuse.
Validate and repair imported geometry (non-manifold edges, degenerate triangles, inverted normals, duplicate vertices) before downstream processing.
Use established interchange formats for runtime delivery and scene composition instead of bespoke binary blobs to stay tool-interoperable.
Compute normals and tangents consistently so lighting matches across content-creation tools and engines.
Build spatial acceleration structures (BVH, k-d tree, octree) for ray queries, nearest-neighbor, and collision rather than brute-force scans.
Keep transforms hierarchical with cached local-to-world matrices and recompute lazily on dirty flags to avoid redundant matrix math.
Stream and level-of-detail large assets (mesh decimation, mipmaps, texture compression) so memory and bandwidth scale with what is on screen.
scaffolds: Scaffold a glTF import/export round-trip tool with material, animation, and node-hierarchy preservation plus a validation pass.
Generate a mesh-processing pipeline: load, weld vertices, recompute normals/tangents, decimate to target LODs, and export.
Build a spatial-index module with ray-cast and nearest-neighbor queries and a small benchmark harness.
Create a procedural-geometry generator emitting validated indexed meshes.

name: Real-Time Rendering & GPU
description: GPU rendering, shaders, and graphics APIs (Vulkan, Metal, DX12, WebGPU) for interactive frame rates.
conventions: Minimize CPU-GPU synchronization and draw-call count: batch by material/pipeline state, use instancing, and prefer indirect/multi-draw over per-object submits.
Use explicit double/triple buffering of per-frame resources so the CPU never stalls waiting on the GPU to finish a prior frame.
Manage GPU memory with a dedicated allocator and sub-allocate from large heaps; avoid per-frame buffer/image creation and destruction.
Make resource state transitions and synchronization explicit and minimal in modern APIs; over-barriering serializes the GPU.
Author shaders against a shared lighting/material convention (linear-space color, energy-conserving PBR) and apply gamma/tonemapping only at output.
Precompile and cache pipeline state objects and shader modules; building them at draw time causes hitches.
Profile on the GPU with timestamp queries and vendor tools and optimize the actual bottleneck (fill rate vs vertex vs bandwidth).
Prefer a render-graph abstraction to declare passes and resource dependencies, enabling automatic barrier and transient-memory management.
scaffolds: Scaffold a cross-platform render loop: swapchain setup, per-frame uniform ring buffer, and a triangle-to-textured-mesh progression.
Generate a forward+ or deferred PBR pipeline skeleton with HDR render targets, tonemapping, and a debug overlay.
Build a minimal render-graph module that declares passes and resources and auto-inserts barriers/transitions.
Create a shader hot-reload plus validation task that recompiles on change and surfaces compile errors inline.

name: Embedded & IoT
description: Firmware for microcontrollers and constrained devices, RTOS tasks, and IoT connectivity.
conventions: Avoid dynamic heap allocation in firmware hot paths and interrupt handlers; prefer static allocation, fixed-size pools, and bounded buffers to keep memory deterministic.
Keep interrupt service routines short: do the minimum, set a flag or push to a queue, and defer real work to a task or main loop.
Use a hardware-abstraction layer so application logic is portable across MCUs and testable on a host without the chip present.
Treat peripheral and shared state accessed from interrupts as volatile and guard against races with critical sections or lock-free single-producer queues.
Budget RAM/flash explicitly and monitor stack high-water marks; stack overflow on MCUs is silent and corrupting without a watchdog/guard.
Enable and feed a hardware watchdog, and design for safe recovery/reset on hang rather than assuming the firmware never wedges.
Manage power deliberately: use sleep/low-power modes, wake on interrupt, and minimize radio/peripheral duty cycle for battery-powered IoT nodes.
Secure IoT connectivity with TLS, provisioned per-device credentials, and signed over-the-air updates with rollback.
scaffolds: Scaffold a bare-metal firmware project with a HAL abstraction, panic handler, and a host-side mock for unit tests.
Generate an RTOS task skeleton: task priorities, a queue between an interrupt and a worker task, and a fed watchdog.
Build an IoT device template: network provisioning, telemetry over a TLS transport, and signed OTA update with rollback.
Create a peripheral driver template with a register-map abstraction, async non-blocking API, and a loopback/integration test.

name: Robotics
description: Robot middleware, motion planning, perception, control, and ROS-based system integration.
conventions: Define and respect a consistent transform tree (frames, parent/child, timestamps) so sensor data, planning, and actuation share one spatial reference.
Timestamp every measurement at the source and fuse using the data's own time, not arrival time, to handle latency and out-of-order sensor streams.
Separate perception, planning, and control into composable nodes/modules with well-defined message interfaces so subsystems are independently testable.
Run control loops at a fixed, guaranteed rate with bounded latency; never block a real-time control path on I/O, logging, or planning.
Model and account for sensor and process noise explicitly with covariances in the estimator rather than treating measurements as exact.
Always implement safety layers: emergency stop, watchdogs, joint/velocity/acceleration limits, and collision checks that can override autonomy.
Validate behavior in a physics simulator with realistic dynamics and sensor models before deploying to hardware.
Record and replay data so failures are reproducible offline and algorithms are evaluated against fixed datasets.
scaffolds: Scaffold a robotics package: node with parameters, a publisher/subscriber pair, a managed lifecycle node, and a launch file plus a sim test.
Generate a motion-planning pipeline: collision environment, planner selection, and trajectory execution with limits.
Build a sensor-fusion estimator template consuming inertial plus odometry/positioning with covariance handling and a replay harness.
Create a SLAM/navigation bring-up: map building, localization, costmaps, and a path-follower with an emergency-stop override.

name: Audio & DSP
description: Real-time audio engines, signal processing, synthesis, and music/audio application development.
conventions: Treat the audio callback as a hard real-time context: never allocate, lock, do I/O, or block in it, since an underrun causes audible glitches.
Communicate between the audio thread and the UI/control threads with lock-free ring buffers or atomic message passing, not mutexes.
Process in blocks/frames sized to the buffer and keep DSP state (filters, delay lines, phase) explicit and reset cleanly on parameter changes.
Be precise about sample rate, channel count, and sample format, and resample correctly when bridging mismatched rates.
Avoid denormals and clamp/limit output to prevent NaNs and clipping; ramp gain and parameter changes to avoid zipper noise and clicks.
Keep latency budgeted and deterministic; report and account for processing latency in the signal chain.
Separate the DSP core from any host/plugin wrapper so the algorithm is unit-testable offline against reference signals.
Validate filters and transforms against analytical responses or reference implementations rather than by ear alone.
scaffolds: Scaffold a real-time audio app with a lock-free callback, a ring-buffer bridge to the UI thread, and block-based processing.
Generate a DSP module template (filter/effect) with explicit state, parameter smoothing, and an offline test against reference signals.
Build an audio plugin skeleton exposing parameters and a testable DSP core separated from the host wrapper.
Create an analysis task: FFT/spectrogram computation with windowing and correct frequency-axis scaling.

name: CLI & Developer Tooling
description: Ergonomic command-line tools, code generators, linters, and developer productivity utilities.
conventions: Follow established CLI conventions: a clear command/subcommand structure, both short and long flags, sensible defaults, and a non-interactive mode for scripting.
Write human-readable output to stdout and diagnostics/errors to stderr, and offer a machine-readable (JSON) output mode for piping and automation.
Return meaningful, documented exit codes so the tool composes correctly in scripts and pipelines.
Make the tool predictable in pipes: detect TTY versus non-TTY, disable color and progress bars when output is not a terminal, and respect NO_COLOR.
Provide thorough --help and shell completions, validate arguments up front, and fail with actionable error messages.
Read configuration with a clear precedence (flags over environment over config file over defaults) and never require interactivity for core operations.
Keep operations idempotent and dry-runnable where they mutate state, and confirm destructive actions or require an explicit flag.
Distribute as a single self-contained binary or well-pinned package, and keep startup fast since CLIs are invoked frequently.
scaffolds: Scaffold a CLI with subcommands, flag parsing, config-file plus environment precedence, and shell completions.
Generate a TUI application skeleton with a model-update-view loop and keyboard navigation.
Build a code-generator command that renders templates from a schema with a dry-run and diff mode.
Create a release setup that cross-compiles single-binary artifacts and publishes them with checksums.

name: Systems Programming
description: Low-level systems software: memory management, concurrency, OS interfaces, and high-performance native code.
conventions: Make ownership and lifetime of every resource explicit; prefer RAII/ownership types so memory, file descriptors, and locks are released deterministically.
Guard all shared mutable state with the right synchronization primitive and document the locking order to prevent deadlocks; prefer message passing or lock-free structures on hot paths.
Avoid undefined behavior: respect aliasing and alignment rules, never read uninitialized memory, and check for integer overflow at boundaries.
Handle every error path explicitly and treat allocation, syscalls, and I/O as fallible; never ignore return codes from system calls.
Minimize allocations in hot paths, design data structures for cache locality, and measure with a profiler before optimizing.
Confine unsafe/low-level code to small, audited, well-documented modules behind safe abstractions, and fuzz/sanitize them.
Use platform abstractions for OS differences rather than scattering conditionals, and check syscall portability assumptions.
Run sanitizers (address, thread, undefined-behavior) and fuzzers in CI to catch memory and concurrency bugs early.
scaffolds: Scaffold a systems project with an ownership-based resource wrapper, error handling, and sanitizer-enabled CI.
Generate a lock-free or bounded concurrent queue with a single-producer/single-consumer fast path and stress tests.
Build a thread-pool / async-runtime task scheduler skeleton with bounded queues and backpressure.
Create a fuzzing harness around a parser or unsafe module wired into CI.

name: Application Security & AppSec
description: Secure coding, vulnerability prevention, secrets management, and security testing across the SDLC.
conventions: Validate and sanitize all input at trust boundaries and encode output for its context to prevent injection and cross-site scripting; use parameterized queries and safe templating rather than string concatenation.
Never hardcode secrets; load them from a secret manager or environment at runtime, rotate them, and scan the repo and history for leaked credentials.
Apply least privilege everywhere (database, cloud IAM, file permissions, tokens) and default to deny, granting only the access a component needs.
Use vetted, current cryptographic libraries and standard algorithms; never roll your own crypto, hash passwords with a slow salted KDF, and keep dependencies patched.
Enforce authentication and authorization on every request server-side; never trust client-side checks, and verify object-level access to prevent insecure direct object references.
Keep dependencies updated and scan them (software composition analysis) plus run static and dynamic analysis in CI to catch known and new vulnerabilities early.
Fail securely: do not leak stack traces, secrets, or internal details in errors, and log security-relevant events without logging sensitive data.
Threat-model new features against a checklist (such as the OWASP Top 10) and add regression tests for fixed vulnerabilities.
scaffolds: Scaffold a security CI pipeline with static analysis, dependency/SCA scanning, secret scanning, and container image scanning.
Generate an input-validation and output-encoding layer for a web endpoint with safe defaults.
Build a secrets-management setup loading credentials from a vault/secret store with rotation hooks.
Create a security regression test suite mapping fixed vulnerabilities to reproducing test cases.

name: Identity, Authentication & Authorization
description: Login, session management, OAuth2/OIDC, and fine-grained authorization in applications.
conventions: Prefer standard protocols (OAuth2, OpenID Connect, SAML) and a vetted identity provider over building authentication from scratch.
Hash passwords with a slow, salted, memory-hard algorithm; never store or log plaintext credentials, and support multi-factor authentication.
Issue short-lived access tokens with refresh tokens, validate token signature, issuer, audience, and expiry on every request, and support revocation.
Store session and refresh tokens securely (httpOnly, secure, same-site cookies for browsers) and protect state-changing requests against cross-site request forgery.
Separate authentication (who you are) from authorization (what you can do); enforce authorization server-side with a clear model (role- or relationship-based).
Apply least privilege and check object-level permissions on every access to prevent privilege escalation and insecure direct object references.
Use the authorization-code flow with PKCE for public clients and never expose client secrets in front-end code.
Rate-limit and monitor authentication endpoints, lock out or step up on suspicious activity, and log auth events for audit without logging secrets.
scaffolds: Scaffold an OIDC/OAuth2 login flow with authorization-code plus PKCE, token validation, and secure session cookies.
Generate a role- or relationship-based authorization layer with policy definitions and middleware enforcement.
Build a multi-factor and password-reset flow with secure token issuance and rate limiting.
Create a token-validation middleware checking signature, issuer, audience, expiry, and revocation.

name: Testing & QA Automation
description: Unit, integration, end-to-end, and property-based testing plus test infrastructure and coverage.
conventions: Follow the test pyramid: many fast, isolated unit tests, fewer integration tests, and a small number of end-to-end tests for critical user journeys.
Make tests deterministic and independent: no shared mutable state, no ordering dependencies, and control time, randomness, and external services with fakes/clocks.
Test behavior and public contracts rather than implementation details so tests survive refactors; assert on observable outcomes, not internals.
Keep tests fast and parallelizable, and eliminate flakiness by waiting on conditions instead of fixed sleeps and by isolating network and filesystem.
Use realistic test data and factories/builders, and prefer real or in-memory dependencies over heavy mocks where feasible to catch integration bugs.
Cover edge cases and invariants with property-based and boundary testing, not just happy-path examples, and add a regression test for every fixed bug.
Treat coverage as a guide, not a goal; prioritize meaningful assertions and critical paths over chasing a coverage percentage.
Run the suite in CI on every change, fail the build on test failures, and quarantine and fix flaky tests rather than retrying blindly.
scaffolds: Scaffold a test harness with unit, integration, and end-to-end layers, fixtures/factories, and CI wiring.
Generate an end-to-end test suite for a critical user journey with condition-based waits and parallel execution.
Build a property-based test template defining invariants and generators for a core module.
Create a contract/integration test setup with in-memory or containerized dependencies and seeded data.

name: Functional Programming
description: Pure functional and typed-functional codebases emphasizing immutability, composition, and effect management.
conventions: Prefer pure functions free of side effects and immutable data structures so behavior is referentially transparent and easy to reason about.
Push side effects to the edges of the system and keep the core logic pure; model effects explicitly with the language's effect types rather than scattering hidden I/O.
Make illegal states unrepresentable with rich, precise types (sum/product types) and exhaustively handle every case.
Compose small, total functions into larger behavior, and prefer transformation pipelines over mutation and imperative loops.
Use higher-order functions, recursion, and lazy evaluation idiomatically, but guard against space leaks and unbounded recursion (use tail calls/accumulators).
Model errors and absence with explicit types (Option/Either/Result) instead of exceptions or nulls, and handle them at the boundary.
Keep functions referentially transparent so they are trivially unit-testable and parallelizable, and lean on property-based testing for laws.
Respect the type-class/algebra laws (functor, monad, monoid) when implementing instances so abstractions behave predictably.
scaffolds: Scaffold a project layout separating a pure core from an effectful boundary with explicit effect types.
Generate an algebraic-data-type model with exhaustive pattern matching and total functions.
Build a transformation pipeline with composed pure functions and property-based law tests.
Create a typed error-handling layer using result/option types threaded through the call chain.

name: Blockchain & Smart Contracts
description: On-chain smart contracts, decentralized apps, and Web3 integrations with a security-first posture.
conventions: Follow the checks-effects-interactions pattern and reentrancy guards so external calls cannot re-enter and drain contract state.
Reuse audited, battle-tested contract libraries for tokens, access control, and upgrades instead of reimplementing primitives.
Validate all inputs and arithmetic, rely on built-in overflow checks, and never trust externally supplied addresses or call data for authorization.
Minimize and explicitly manage on-chain state and gas; mark visibility and mutability precisely and avoid unbounded loops over user-controlled data.
Make contracts deterministic and avoid dangerous randomness/oracle assumptions; use proven oracle and price-feed patterns rather than spot values.
Design upgradeability deliberately (proxy patterns) with strict access control and timelocks, since deployed code is immutable and bugs are unrecoverable without a plan.
Test exhaustively with unit, fuzz, and invariant tests plus fork tests against mainnet state, and obtain an independent audit before mainnet deployment.
Keep private keys and deployment credentials in secure signers/hardware, never in code, and protect admin keys with multisig.
scaffolds: Scaffold a smart-contract project with a build/test toolchain, audited base contracts, and fuzz/invariant tests.
Generate a token or access-control contract extending audited libraries with role-based permissions.
Build a typed front-end integration that connects a wallet, reads state, and sends transactions with error handling.
Create an upgradeable proxy setup with timelocked admin controls and a deployment script using a secure signer.

name: Distributed Systems & Consensus
description: Replicated, fault-tolerant services with consensus, partitioning, and consistency trade-offs.
conventions: Design for partial failure: assume any node, link, or disk can fail at any time, and make every operation retryable and idempotent so retries are safe.
Choose consistency deliberately along the CAP/PACELC trade-off and document the guarantees (linearizable, sequential, eventual) each operation provides.
Use a proven consensus algorithm (Raft/Paxos) or an existing coordination service for leader election and replicated state rather than inventing one.
